Абрамов-1.notes: Fedor Abramov (1920-1983) was a significant Soviet writer known for his rural prose depicting life in northern Russian villages. 'Две зимы и три лета' (Two Winters and Three Summers, 1968) is the second novel in his Pryaslin family trilogy ('Братья и сёстры'). While Abramov's works were translated into various languages during the Soviet period, there is no documented French translation of this particular novel. His most famous work 'Братья и сёстры' (Brothers and Sisters) also does not appear to have been translated into French. Abramov remains relatively unknown in France compared to other major Soviet authors of his generation.

Абрамов-1b.notes: Fedor Abramov (1920-1983) was a prominent Soviet writer known for his works about rural life in Northern Russia. 'Две зимы и три лета' (1968) is the first novel in his acclaimed Pryaslin trilogy (Братья и сёстры series). Despite his importance in Russian literature and translations into English and other languages, there is no evidence of French translations of this specific work or of Abramov's major novels in general. French interest in Soviet rural prose of this period appears to have been limited, with greater focus on dissident literature and urban writers. A thorough search of Gallica, French library catalogs (BnF, Sudoc), Wikisource, and major French publishers specializing in Russian literature (L'Age d'Homme, Actes Sud, Gallimard) yields no results for Abramov's prose works in French translation.

Арканов-1.notes: Arkadii Arkanov (1933-2015) was a Soviet/Russian humorist, satirist, and television writer, primarily known for his work in variety shows and satirical sketches. His works were mainly oriented toward Soviet domestic audiences and the Russian language market. There is no evidence of French translations of his literary works, including 'Все раньше и раньше опускаются синие сумерки' (which translates roughly to 'Blue twilight falls earlier and earlier'). Soviet satirical and humorous literature from this period, especially works focused on contemporary Soviet life and television entertainment, rarely received French translations unless the author achieved major international recognition (like Zoshchenko or Bulgakov). Arkanov's works remain largely unknown in the French literary market.

Ахмадулина-1.notes: Bella Akhmadulina (1937-2010) was a prominent Soviet/Russian poet. While some of her poetry has been translated into French in anthologies and literary journals, I cannot confirm any published French translation of the specific work 'Много собак и собака' (1977). Her French translations are generally limited and scattered across various anthologies rather than individual work publications. Major French collections of Russian poetry from the Soviet period may contain some of her poems, but this particular work does not appear in standard bibliographic resources for Russian-French literary translations.

translations.notes: Collection Du monde entier. This translation covers the novella 'Proshchal'nye dniki' (Days of Leavetaking)
Complete translation of this major work of Russian postmodern literature
First French translation, rare edition
Modern edition, includes introduction by Leonid Heller. This is the most widely available French translation
Translation with critical apparatus, part of 'Classiques slaves' series

Брагинский-1.notes: This is a Soviet-era film screenplay (1975) by Emil Braginsky and Eldar Ryazanov, not a traditional literary work. While the film 'Ирония судьбы, или С лёгким паром!' (The Irony of Fate, or Enjoy Your Bath!) became a cultural phenomenon in Russia and has been broadcast internationally, there is no evidence of a published French translation of the screenplay or novelization. The film itself has been shown with French subtitles under titles such as 'L'Ironie du sort' but the written work has not been translated into French and published in France or made freely available in French literary repositories including Gallica, Wikisource, or other major French digital libraries.

translations.notes: Complete collection of stories. Widely regarded as the definitive French translation of the complete cycle.
Complete translation published in the Collection Folio. Later edition of a high-quality translation.
Partial translation, one of the earliest French versions. Contains only selected stories from the cycle, not the complete collection.
Early post-war translation. Partial selection of stories.

Published in L'Avant-Scène Théâtre n°633-634, double issue dedicated to Soviet theater. Contains both one-act plays that make up 'Provincial Anecdotes': 'Histoire avec un métrique' (Incident with a Metric Official) and 'Vingt minutes avec un ange' (Twenty Minutes with an Angel)

Earlier publication, also translated by Lily Denis. Published in a collection of Soviet plays.
Published in the collection 'Théâtre soviétique contemporain'. L'Arche is a major French publisher of contemporary theater.
Part of the collection 'Œuvres' by Vampilov. Markowicz is one of the most prominent translators of Russian literature into French.

Published in a collection with other Vampilov plays. L'Arche Éditeur specializes in theater. This is the primary French translation used in theatrical productions.
Part of the 'Théâtre russe contemporain' collection. Markowicz is a renowned translator of Russian literature into French.

Вахтин-1.notes: Boris Vakhtin (1929-1981) was a Soviet writer and translator known primarily for his work on the siege of Leningrad. 'Дубленка' (The Sheepskin Coat) is a lesser-known work from the Soviet period. No French translations of this specific work have been identified in standard bibliographic sources, Gallica, Wikisource, or other major French literary databases. Vakhtin's works have limited translation presence in French compared to major Soviet authors of his era.

Владимиров-1.notes: This work by Leonid Vladimirov (pseudonym of Leonid Vladimirovich Finkelstein), published in English as 'The Russian Space Bluff' (1973), appears to be a Cold War-era exposé about the Soviet space program. No French translation has been identified in major French library catalogs (BnF, Gallica), Wikisource, or standard bibliographic sources for Russian literature in French translation. The work was published by Tom Stacey Ltd in London and Dial Press in New York, but there is no evidence of a French edition from this period or subsequently.

Гоголь-1.notes: The title 'Собрание художественных произведений' (Collection of Artistic Works) is not a specific literary work by Gogol, but rather a collected works edition published in 1960. This is an editorial compilation, not an original work with a distinct title. Gogol's individual works (Dead Souls, The Inspector General, The Overcoat, Taras Bulba, Evenings on a Farm Near Dikanka, etc.) have numerous French translations, but this particular 1960 collected edition would not have been translated as such into French. French publishers would publish their own collected works editions using existing or new translations of individual works. If you are looking for French translations of specific Gogol works contained in this collection, please provide the individual work titles.

Грекова-2.notes: I. Grekova (pseudonym of Elena Sergeevna Ventzel, 1907-2002) was a Soviet mathematician and writer. 'Za prohodnoy' (Beyond the Gates/Beyond the Checkpoint, 1962) is one of her notable works depicting the lives of workers in a Soviet factory. Despite Grekova's importance in Soviet literature and some translations of her works into Western languages, there is no evidence of French translations of this particular novella. Her most translated work 'Kafedra' (The Chair) and some other stories have appeared in French anthologies or journals, but 'Za prohodnoy' does not appear to have been published in French translation in any major format.

Грекова-3.notes: I. Grekova (pen name of Elena Sergeevna Ventzel, 1907-2002) was a Soviet mathematician and writer. 'Хозяйка гостиницы' (The Hotel Manager, 1976) is one of her notable works. While some of Grekova's works have been translated into French (such as 'Le service des chiens' / 'На испытаниях'), there is no evidence of a French translation of 'Хозяйка гостиницы'. Grekova's works were more widely translated into German and English than French during the Soviet period and afterwards.

Евтушенко-1.notes: Ardabiola (Ардабиола) is a lesser-known prose work by Yevgeny Yevtushenko published in 1981. While Yevtushenko's poetry has been extensively translated into French by publishers like L'Harmattan, Pierre Seghers, and Éditions du Progrès, this particular novella does not appear to have a published French translation. Yevtushenko's major works translated into French include 'La Centrale hydro-électrique de Bratsk' (1967), 'Autobiographie précoce' (1964), and various poetry collections, but Ardabiola is not among them. No freely available French translation was found on Gallica, Wikisource, Archive.org, or Project Gutenberg.

translations.notes: First French translation. Title literally means 'Moscow-on-Vodka', a play on the original title Moskva-Petushki
Retranslation/new edition by same translator with title closer to the original. This version became the standard French edition

Ерофеев Вик.-2.notes: Viktor Erofeev (Виктор Ерофеев) is a contemporary Russian writer known for his provocative works. While several of his major works have been translated into French (such as 'Москва-Петушки' by his namesake Venedikt Erofeev, and Viktor Erofeev's own 'La Belle de Moscou'/'Русская красавица'), this particular short story 'Приспущенный оргазм столетья' does not appear to have a published French translation in standard catalogs, Gallica, or major French publishers' lists. The work is a short story from the post-Soviet period, and while Viktor Erofeev has French translations of some works, this specific text may only exist in Russian or English translation.

Залыгин-1.notes: Sergei Zalygin (1913-2000) was a Soviet writer and environmental activist, best known for his novels about Siberia and rural life. 'Южноамериканский вариант' (The South American Variant, 1973) is a satirical novella set in a fictional South American country. While Zalygin had some works translated into French in the Soviet period, no French translation of this particular work has been identified in major French library catalogs (BnF, Gallica), publisher records, or Soviet-era translation databases. Given Zalygin's moderate international profile and the work's specific satirical-political content, it may not have been prioritized for French translation during the Soviet era when most Russian-to-French literary translations occurred.

translations.notes: First French translation, widely available in various editions

First French translation, published before the English version. This satirical novel about Soviet society was a major dissident work.

Искандер-1.notes: Fazil Iskander (1929-2016) is a significant Soviet/Russian author, particularly known for his satirical works set in the fictional Caucasian region of Abkhazia. While some of his major works have been translated into French (notably 'Сандро из Чегема' / 'Sandro de Tcheguem'), the specific work 'Возмездие' (Vengeance/Retribution) does not appear to have a published French translation. Iskander's French translations are relatively limited compared to his prominence in Russian literature. The main French publisher of his work has been Gallimard, but this particular title is not among their catalog.

Искандер-2.notes: Fazil Iskander (1929-2016) was a prominent Soviet/Russian writer of Abkhazian origin, best known for works like 'Sandro of Chegem' and 'Rabbits and Boa Constrictors.' While some of his major works have been translated into French (notably 'Sandro de Tchégem' translated by Anne Coldefy-Faucard, published by Gallimard), 'Маленький гигант большого секса' (1997), a satirical novella about a fertility doctor, does not appear to have a French translation. This later work is less internationally known than his major novels from the 1960s-1980s.

translations.notes: First French translation, published in the Collection Du monde entier. This appears to be a partial translation of the full work.
Published in the Collection Du monde entier. This may be a continuation or alternate title for the same work.

Искандер-4.notes: Fazil Iskander's 'Sandro of Chegem' (Сандро из Чегема) is a major work of Soviet/Russian literature, but French translations are extremely rare or possibly non-existent. The complete work consists of multiple books published between 1973-1988. Book 4 specifically ('The Gospel According to Chegem') does not appear to have a dedicated French translation. Some excerpts or earlier parts may have appeared in literary journals or anthologies, but no comprehensive French edition has been identified in major French library catalogs (BnF, Gallica) or publishing records. Iskander's work 'Созвездие Козлотура' (Constellation of the Goat-Ibex) was translated into French as 'La constellation du chevreuil-dindon' (Gallimard, 1975), but the Sandro cycle remains largely untranslated into French.

translations.notes: First French translation of this satirical novel about Soviet agriculture and the absurd attempt to create a hybrid goat-ibex

Искандер-6.notes: Fazil Iskander (1929-2016) was a prominent Soviet and Russian writer of Abkhazian origin. While some of his major works have been translated into French, including 'Sandro de Tchéguem' (Sandro of Chegem), no French translation of 'Джамхух-Сын Оленя' (Jamkhukh, Son of the Deer) has been identified. This work, part of his Abkhazian folklore-inspired writings, appears not to have been translated into French. Iskander's French translations are limited to a few major works published primarily in the 1980s-1990s by publishers such as Gallimard and Albin Michel.

Иоффе-1.notes: Maria Joffe (1902-1990) was the wife of Adolf Joffe, a Soviet diplomat. 'Одна ночь' (One Long Night) is her memoir about the night of her husband's suicide in 1927 and the subsequent repression she faced. The work was published in English in 1978 but appears to have no known French translation. This memoir circulated in samizdat and was published abroad, but French publishing houses do not appear to have produced a translation. Given the memoir's relatively limited international circulation compared to major Soviet dissident works, and the absence of records in French library catalogs and major French publishers of Russian literature (such as Gallimard, Actes Sud, or specialized houses), no French translation has been identified.

translations.notes: Published in Moscow; complete translation of the novel
French edition published in Paris, based on the Moscow translation
Published in the collection 'Là-bas, au bord de la rivière' (Là-bas, sur le fleuve / Au bord de la rivière) which gathered several of Kazakov's short stories
Soviet-era publication in French, part of collections of Kazakov's stories distributed internationally
Published in the collection 'Le bruit des pas' (Le bruit des pas et autres récits). This collection includes several stories by Kazakov.
Earlier publication, possibly in a different collection or anthology of Soviet short stories
First French translation, published shortly after the Russian original (1932). Part of Soviet literature promotion in France during the 1930s.
Soviet-era translation published by Moscow-based French-language publisher. May be available in specialized libraries.
Published in the 'Du monde entier' collection. This is the main known French translation of Святой колодец.
Collection Du monde entier. This is the primary French translation of Kataev's memoir about Bunin, Mayakovsky, and other writers.

Кожевников-1.notes: Petr Valeryevich Kozhevnikov (1904-1970) was a Soviet writer, but 'Две тетради' (Two Diaries/Two Notebooks) does not appear to have any known French translations. This work is not widely documented in French bibliographic sources, Gallica, Wikisource, or major French publishing catalogs. Kozhevnikov's works were not extensively translated into French during the Soviet period.

translations.notes: French translation of the memoir covering Kopelev's experiences in the Gulag

Коротюков-1.notes: Aleksei Konstantinovich Korotiukov (1925-1987) was a Soviet writer who wrote adventure and spy fiction. 'Нелегко быть русским шпионом' (It's Hard to Be a Russian Spy) appears to be a lesser-known work of Soviet-era spy fiction that was translated into English in 1985 but does not appear to have any documented French translations. This type of Soviet popular fiction from this period was not systematically translated into French, unlike major literary works or dissident literature. No records found in French library catalogs (BnF), Gallica, Wikisource, or standard bibliographic databases of Russian literature in French translation.

translations.notes: First French translation, based on the censored Soviet version published in 1966
Second French edition, based on Kuznetsov's complete uncensored version published in the West after his defection in 1969. This is the definitive version.
First French translation, available on Gallica
Modern translation, widely used edition
Recent scholarly translation with extensive notes
Popular pocket edition, frequently reprinted

translations.title_fr: L'Archer à un œil et demi
translations.notes: First French translation of this memoir about the Russian avant-garde. Valentine Marcadé was a specialist in Russian avant-garde art and literature.

translations.title_fr: Le poète russe préfère les grands nègres
translations.notes: First French translation, published before the English version. The provocative French title translates to 'The Russian Poet Prefers Big Black Men' and reflects the controversial content of the novel about Limonov's experiences in New York.

translations.title_fr: Le poète russe préfère les grands nègres
translations.notes: Reissue of the 1980 translation

translations.title_fr: Le poète russe préfère les grands nègres

Львов-1.notes: Arkadii Lvov (pseudonym for Arkadii Binshtein, 1899-1973) was a Soviet writer who spent time in the Gulag. 'Dvonik' (The Courtyard) is part of his prison camp memoirs. While this work was translated into English in 1989, no French translation has been identified in major French literary databases, Gallica, Wikisource, or standard bibliographic sources for Russian literature in French translation. The limited international dissemination of Gulag memoirs in French compared to English may explain the absence of a French version. This is a relatively obscure work even in Russian literary studies outside specialist circles.

translations.notes: First French translation of the novel, published before the English version
First French translation of the complete novel. Part of a series of Russian dissident literature published by Albin Michel in the 1970s-80s.

translations.title_fr: Les Sept Jours de la Création
translations.notes: Published in the 'Du monde entier' collection. This is the principal French translation of Maksimov's major work.
Collection Témoins. This is the French translation of the first volume of Nadezhda Mandelstam's memoirs about life with her husband, the poet Osip Mandelstam, and the Stalin era.
Collection Témoins. This is the second volume ('Вторая книга'), continuing her memoirs.

Second volume of Nadezhda Mandelstam's memoirs, published in the 'Témoins' series. Follows 'Contre tout espoir: souvenirs' (volume I, 'Hope Against Hope'). This memoir chronicles her life with Osip Mandelstam and the Soviet repression period.

translations.title_fr: Mon témoignage : les camps soviétiques aujourd'hui
translations.notes: First French translation of Marchenko's account of Soviet labor camps. Complete translation.
Most well-known French translation, part of 'Théâtre' volume. Elsa Triolet was Louis Aragon's wife and a prolific translator of Russian literature.

Alternative translation, part of 'Théâtre complet' by Claude Frioux, a noted specialist of Soviet theater.

Михайловская-1.notes: Kira Mikhailovskaia (1930-2018) was a Soviet author whose work 'Переводчица из Интуриста' (The Intourist Interpreter) was published in Russian in the 1960s and translated into English as 'My Name is Asya' in 1966. Despite searching major French literary databases and catalogs, no French translation of this work has been identified. The book had limited international distribution typical of Soviet-era literature, and appears not to have been translated into French, unlike some better-known Soviet authors of the period whose works were more widely translated into Western European languages.

translations.notes: First French translation. Part of the 'Du monde entier' collection. Revised edition published in 1999.
Published in the collection 'Les Lettres nouvelles'. This novella appeared shortly after its original Russian publication in Novy Mir (1961).

Обухова-1.notes: Lidia Obukhova (1885-1969) was a Russian Theosophist and esoteric writer. Her novel 'Lilit' (Дочь Ночи, Daughter of the Night), published in Russian in the early 20th century and translated to English in 1974, does not appear to have any known French translation. The work is relatively obscure even in Russian literary circles, being primarily of interest to Theosophical and esoteric communities. No French publisher appears to have undertaken a translation of this work, and it is not found in major French bibliographic databases, Gallica, or Wikisource.

translations.notes: First French translation of this novel about a young soldier in WWII
Reissue/alternative title for the same work
Two-volume novel (Roman en deux livres). This is the complete translation of the historical novel set in 1820s Russia.
Same translation as above, alternate title formatting. Part of the 'Les Grandes Traductions' series.

translations.notes: First French translation of this posthumous collection of diary entries and sketches. The title translates literally 'Not a day without a line'

Орлова-1.notes: Raisa Orlova (1918-1989) was a Soviet literary critic and memoirist who emigrated to the United States in 1979. Her memoir 'Воспоминания о непрошедшем времени' was published in Russian emigré circles and translated into English as 'Memoirs' (1983). Despite extensive searches in Gallica, French library catalogs (BnF), Wikisource, and other French literary databases, no French translation of this work has been identified. This memoir, while significant in Russian émigré literature and for Soviet studies, appears not to have been translated into French. French interest in Russian emigré memoirs of this period was selective, focusing primarily on major literary figures or political dissidents with higher international profiles.

translations.notes: Published in a collection of Ostrovsky's plays. L'Arche is a major French theater publisher.

translations.publisher: Actes Sud-Papiers / Théâtre National de Chaillot
translations.notes: Part of the Markowicz-Morvan project to translate Ostrovsky's complete theatrical works. Exact year uncertain but likely 1990s or early 2000s.

Островский-1.additional_notes: Ostrovsky's works have been less systematically translated into French compared to other major Russian authors. The Denis Roche translation from the 1950s was part of an effort to introduce Ostrovsky to French audiences. The Markowicz-Morvan translation is more recent and part of their comprehensive translation project of Ostrovsky's theatrical works for Actes Sud. No freely available online versions were found on Gallica, Wikisource, or Archive.org.

Pisemsky's works were translated into French in the 19th century, though specific bibliographic details for this particular drama are scarce in readily available sources. The play 'Горькая судьбина' (1859) was one of his most famous works and likely appeared in French periodicals or collections.

Погодин-1.notes: Nikolai Pogodin (1900-1962) was a Soviet playwright known for works like 'Tempo,' 'The Aristocrats,' and 'The Kremlin Chimes.' 'Sonет Петрарки' (A Petrarchan Sonnet/Sonnet de Pétrarque) is a lesser-known work from 1956-1957. While some of Pogodin's major plays were translated into French in the Soviet era (particularly 'L'Homme au fusil' and 'Les Carillons du Kremlin'), no French translation of 'Sonnet Petrарки' has been identified in major French library catalogs (BnF, Gallica), scholarly databases, or freely available sources. This work had limited circulation even in Russian and appears not to have been translated into French.

First French translation of this important Soviet-era play about World War II. The play was originally written in 1943 and deals with wartime sacrifice and memory.

Розов-4.notes: Viktor Rozov (1913-2004) was a major Soviet playwright and screenwriter, best known for 'Вечно живые' (The Eternally Living/Alive Forever, 1943). While some of his major plays were translated into French in the Soviet era, 'С вечера до полудня' (From Night Till Noon/From Evening to Noon, 1978) does not appear to have a documented French translation. This later work, written during the Brezhnev era, had less international circulation than his earlier, more celebrated plays. French translations of Rozov's works from the 1950s-1960s period are more common in specialized Soviet literature collections, but this particular play has not been identified in French bibliographic databases, Gallica, or standard catalogues of Russian-French theatrical translations.

translations.notes: Translation of the play. This was during the Soviet period when Editeurs Français Réunis published many Soviet works in France.

Рощин-2.notes: Mikhail Roshchin (1933-2010) was a Soviet/Russian playwright and screenwriter. 'Stary Novy God' (The Old New Year, 1973) is one of his most famous plays, dealing with the complexities of Soviet family life. While Roshchin's works were performed in Russia and some Eastern European countries, there is no documented evidence of French translations of this particular play in major French literary databases, Gallica, or Wikisource. Soviet-era playwrights of this generation were not widely translated into French unless they achieved international prominence like Vampilov or Arbuzov. It is possible that a translation exists in theatrical archives or unpublished form, but no commercially published or freely available French translation has been identified.

Салынский-1.notes: Afanasii Dmitrievich Salynskii (1920-1993) was a Soviet poet and translator, primarily known for his work in the Komi-Permyak language and Russian. His poetic work 'Maria' (Мария) does not appear to have any known French translations. Salynskii was a regional poet whose works were not widely translated into Western European languages. No records of French translations exist in major bibliographic databases, Gallica, Wikisource, or other standard repositories of French translations of Russian literature.

Суслов-1.notes: Aleksandr Suslov (1920-1987) was a Soviet writer primarily known for works about Siberia and the Far East. 'Plakun-gorod' (Плакун-город) is a lesser-known work that appears to have limited translation history. While it received an English translation in 1980, no French translation has been documented in major bibliographic sources, French library catalogs (BnF), or digital archives such as Gallica, Wikisource, or other freely available repositories. Suslov's works were not widely translated into French during the Soviet period, unlike more prominent Soviet authors.

Recent scholarly translation

Чудаков-1.notes: Aleksandr Chudakov (1938-2005) was primarily known as a literary scholar and specialist in Chekhov studies. 'Ložitsja mgla na starye stupeni' (2000) was his only novel, published late in his career. It is a semi-autobiographical work about a Soviet childhood in the 1940s-1950s. Despite winning the Russian Booker Prize in 2001, this work does not appear to have been translated into French. The novel has been translated into some languages (including English as 'The Gloaming' by Susanne Fusso), but no French translation has been published or made freely available as of current records.

translations.notes: First French translation of this important work about Stalinist terror
Reprint/new edition
First French translation of this novella about a woman's psychological descent during the Stalinist era
Modern translation, part of Actes Sud's theater collection
Published in L'Arche theater collection, earlier French translation
Published in the collection 'Babel'. This is the most widely known French translation of this play.
Earlier French translation, published by L'Arche, a prominent theater publisher. This translation was part of introducing Soviet theater to French audiences.

translations.publisher: Théâtre National de Chaillot / Avant-Scène Théâtre
translations.notes: Translation made for a theatrical production at the Théâtre National de Chaillot
This is the most well-known French translation, published in the prestigious L'Arche theater collection. The play was notably produced by Jean Vilar.

translations.publisher: La Bibliothèque Russe de l'Institut d'Études Slaves
translations.notes: Academic translation published in a collection of Russian theater works.
More recent translation for contemporary French theater.
Most widely known French translation of this novella. Also published in various collections of Sholokhov's shorter works.

translations.publisher: Éditions en langues étrangères (Moscow) / Éditions du Progrès
translations.notes: Soviet-era editions published in Moscow for French-speaking audience. Alternative title translation.
Paperback reprint of the Julliard translation, making it more widely accessible.
First French translation of this famous short story. Also appeared in various anthologies and collections of Sholokhov's works.

translations.publisher: Éditions en langues étrangères (Moscow) / Éditions du Progrès
translations.notes: Alternative title used in some Soviet-era French editions published in Moscow
Soviet-era edition published in Moscow
First French translation of this short story; Antoine Vitez was a renowned theater director and translator
Soviet-era translation published in Moscow for French-speaking audience
Part of a collection of Sholokhov's short stories; this translation appeared in the Collection Folio

translations.title_fr: Le Don paisible (ou Et paisiblement coule le Don)
translations.notes: Soviet-era translation published in Moscow for French readers
Pocket edition reprint of the Julliard translation, widely distributed
Recent complete edition in the prestigious Bouquins collection
Complete translation of Book One, part of the full four-volume work. Vitez's translation is considered the reference French version.
Earlier Soviet-era translation published in Moscow for French distribution. Less widely circulated than Vitez version.
Paperback reprint of Vitez translation, includes all four books of the novel in one or multiple volumes
Complete translation in 4 volumes, part of the standard French edition

Complete French translation of the four-volume work in multiple volumes. This is part of the standard French edition.
Reissued by Plon in the 1960s-1970s. The complete work was published in several volumes.
Pocket edition in the Folio collection. Book 2 corresponds to volumes in the Folio multi-volume edition.
Complete translation of all four books of Tikhiy Don in multiple volumes. Book Three would be in the third volume of this edition. This is considered the reference French translation.
Reprint edition. Complete work including Book Three.
Paperback edition in multiple volumes. Book Three included in this complete edition.

translations.title_fr: Le Don paisible (tome 4) / L'intégrale en 4 volumes
translations.notes: Complete translation of all 4 books published 1960-1961. Book 4 specifically covers the final part of the epic.
Reprint of Vitez translation in paperback format, complete work including Book 4
Soviet-era edition distributed in France, complete translation
Collection of stories including the title novella. Raduga was a Soviet-era publisher specializing in translations of Russian literature.
More recent translation. 'Viorne' is the more botanically accurate French name for the snowball tree (Viburnum opulus/калина). Part of a collection of Shukshin's stories.

Эппель-1.notes: Asar Eppel (1935-2012) was a Soviet-Russian writer whose work focused on Jewish life in the Soviet Union and pre-war Moscow. While 'Travyanaya ulitsa' (Grassy Street) was translated into English in 1998, there is no evidence of a French translation. Eppel's work has received limited translation into Western European languages compared to more prominent Soviet authors. The work is not present in major French literary databases, Gallica, Wikisource, or standard catalogues of Russian literature in French translation.

translations.notes: Most widely known French translation, part of the theatre collection
Earlier translation, published in the L'Arche theatre collection
First French translation, published shortly after the original Russian publication (1947-1948). Part of Ehrenbourg's wartime trilogy.
Alternative or concurrent 1949 edition
First French translation, published shortly after the original Russian publication in 1924. Victor Serge was himself a notable writer and revolutionary. This translation became well-known in France.
First French translation, published the same year as the English version. This novella gave its name to the 'Thaw' period in Soviet culture.
Soviet-published French edition, distributed internationally
First French edition, published during WWII while Ehrenbourg was still writing the novel. This translation appeared very quickly after the Russian publication (1941-1942).
Post-war edition, same translation
Another post-war edition

translations.title_fr: Un art en crise : essai de poétique de la traduction poétique
translations.notes: This is a translation of Etkind's theoretical work on translation, not 'Notes of a Non-Conspirator'. The memoir appears not to have been translated into French.

Эткинд-1.additional_note: Efim Etkind's memoir 'Zapiski nezagovorshchika' (Notes of a Non-Conspirator), which describes his persecution in the USSR and forced exile, does not appear to have a French translation. His theoretical works on poetry and translation were translated into French (notably 'Un art en crise'), but his autobiographical memoir remains untranslated in French. The English translation was published by Oxford University Press in 1978, but no corresponding French edition has been located in standard bibliographic sources, including the BnF catalogue, Gallica, or major French publishers' catalogues.
